case_name: Balancing Mirbane oil, MTBE, and diuron control from brackish groundwater for high-recovery potable reuse
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for brackish groundwater containing nitrobenzene, MTBE, and diuron intended for potable reuse, while balancing organic removal performance against high water recovery and limited concentrate handling options.

input_data.scenario_background: A coastal inland-reuse utility plans to upgrade a brackish groundwater source impacted by legacy agricultural herbicide runoff, fuel oxygenate release, and historical Mirbane oil contamination. The finished water will be blended into a potable reuse supply, so organics control must be reliable, but the site has limited options for off-site brine disposal and a strong preference for high overall water recovery. Several technically feasible routes exist, including adsorption-focused treatment and membrane desalting, but the preferred choice must balance recovery, concentrate burden, salinity management, and operator practicality rather than simple contaminant removability alone.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of nitrobenzene, MTBE, and diuron to support potable reuse compliance and product water quality expectations, while also reducing salinity to a level suitable for blending into the reuse train.
secondary_goals: maintain stable performance under moderate source variability

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: concentrate disposal capacity is limited and costly, so treatment trains that create large brine volumes are disadvantaged unless clearly justified
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: must tolerate seasonal salinity shifts and moderate contaminant fluctuations without frequent process upsets

core_unit_functions.oxidant_quench_and_guard_filtration: protect downstream adsorption and membrane units from particulates and oxidant damage
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_with_offgas_treatment: remove a large fraction of volatile MTBE before adsorption and desalting
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: adsorb diuron and nitrobenzene and polish residual organics including remaining MTBE
core_unit_functions.low_pressure_reverse_osmosis: reduce TDS for potable reuse blending while operating at relatively high practical recovery for brackish groundwater
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for finished water compatibility
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse integration

applicability_rationale: this hybrid route separates treatment duties so volatile MTBE is reduced before GAC, lowering carbon loading and replacement frequency
nitrobenzene and diuron are well suited to adsorption polishing, helping control trace organic risk before and after desalting
using low-pressure brackish RO after bulk organic removal can achieve salinity reduction with less fouling and lower organic passage risk than RO alone

compared with full organic removal by membranes alone, this configuration better supports high water recovery because a portion of contaminant control occurs without generating additional concentrate mass
the train balances potable reuse expectations with the site's concentrate constraint better than more membrane-heavy alternatives
potential_risks: air stripping is less effective for MTBE than for many petroleum hydrocarbons, so tower design and polishing capacity must be conservative
GAC changeout frequency may increase if DOC or herbicide loading rises seasonally
RO still generates a brine stream and recovery will be limited by scaling control and concentrate management capacity
off-gas treatment is needed to prevent transfer of volatile contamination from water to air

core_unit_functions.guard_filtration: remove suspended solids and protect oxidation and membrane units
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_h2o2: oxidize MTBE and partially transform recalcitrant trace organics
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon: adsorb residual nitrobenzene and diuron and biologically polish oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.low_pressure_reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved salts for potable reuse blending
core_unit_functions.stabilization: condition permeate for distribution or blending

applicability_rationale: AOP can be attractive where MTBE is a major driver because oxidation may reduce reliance on air transfer and can complement downstream BAC polishing
the BAC step helps manage oxidation byproducts and provides additional removal of aromatic and herbicide contaminants
RO remains useful for TDS reduction required by the brackish source
this route is technically strong for broad organic control but ranks below the top option because AOP increases energy use, chemical demand, and process complexity
potential_risks: AOP may have variable treatment efficiency for MTBE depending on radical demand and water matrix
bromide in the source creates concern for bromate formation if oxidation chemistry is not carefully controlled
BAC requires monitoring to avoid breakthrough or biological instability
RO concentrate management remains necessary

core_unit_functions.guard_filtration: protect downstream adsorbers and ion-transport equipment
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_lead_lag: remove nitrobenzene, diuron, and a portion of MTBE through staged adsorption
core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_reversal: reduce salinity from brackish groundwater with potential for higher water recovery than RO in suitable matrices

applicability_rationale: EDR is attractive under high-water-recovery and concentrate-constraint conditions because brackish desalting can sometimes be achieved with better recovery flexibility than RO
placing GAC ahead of EDR addresses the nonionic organic contaminants that EDR would not reliably remove
for moderate TDS brackish groundwater, this route can materially reduce concentrate volume while still meeting salinity goals

the option ranks below the top two because trace organics removal relies heavily on carbon, MTBE adsorption is less favorable than for more hydrophobic compounds, and EDR integration is less common for potable reuse trace-organic scenarios
potential_risks: GAC may exhibit earlier MTBE breakthrough than for nitrobenzene or diuron
EDR does not directly solve removal of neutral organic micropollutants, so pretreatment and carbon management are critical
scaling and membrane stack maintenance must be actively controlled in hard brackish groundwater
regulatory acceptance for potable reuse may favor more established barrier combinations

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_softening_and_filtration: control scaling and fouling before high-rejection membranes
core_unit_functions.two_pass_reverse_osmosis: provide strong dissolved solids and organic rejection
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust product water chemistry after aggressive desalting
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: provide additional oxidation barrier for trace organics in permeate
core_unit_functions.post_GAC: polish any remaining organics and oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: this route is technically capable and offers very strong finished-water quality for potable reuse
multiple barriers can be appealing when compliance conservatism is the top priority
however, the train is downgraded because it is misaligned with the site's high-recovery and concentrate-handling constraints
two-pass RO plus AOP and post-GAC creates high capital cost, high energy use, significant operator burden, and substantial brine management demand
potential_risks: large concentrate volume and elevated disposal cost conflict with site limitations
overall recovery may be lower than desired once scaling, cleaning, and second-pass operation are accounted for
complex process control and membrane maintenance requirements are substantial
over-treatment may not be economically justified for this source water

core_unit_functions.guard_filtration: protect adsorption and ion-exchange units from particulate fouling
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: remove nitrobenzene and diuron and polish residual organics
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange: provide additional dissolved constituent reduction and polishing of selected anionic species

applicability_rationale: this low-brine route is appealing from a recovery standpoint because it avoids membrane concentrate generation
air stripping and GAC can technically address the named organics if well designed
it ranks last because it does not adequately address the brackish salinity burden for potable reuse blending in this scenario
anion exchange is not a primary solution for the neutral target organics and does not replace the need for robust TDS reduction
potential_risks: finished water salinity may remain too high for the intended potable reuse role
MTBE polishing on GAC may be inconsistent and carbon usage could be high
anion exchange waste brine or regenerant handling may still be required depending on resin strategy
the train may fail overall water-quality objectives even if the named organics are controlled

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains that combine targeted organic removal with moderate-recovery desalting should rank near the top
membrane-only or membrane-heavy routes should not rank first when concentrate handling is strongly constrained
an option that avoids concentrate but fails salinity objectives should appear lower despite favorable recovery

must_reflect: high water recovery preference for a brackish groundwater application
limited tolerance for large brine or difficult concentrate disposal
need to treat both trace organic contaminants and background salinity for potable reuse
trade-offs between stronger treatment barriers and operational simplicity

must_contain: why MTBE can be more difficult for adsorption-only treatment than nitrobenzene or diuron
why brackish salinity pushes desalting into the decision even though the named contaminants are organics
why concentrate generation downgrades otherwise technically strong RO-centered options
why hybrid pretreatment can reduce organic loading and improve downstream desalting fit

should_mention: MTBE breakthrough or incomplete volatilization risk
off-gas handling requirements for stripping-based options
RO or EDR scaling and concentrate management
bromate or oxidation byproduct concerns for AOP routes
carbon replacement frequency and spent media handling
